ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: To Eulogius and Anastasius, Bishops.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to Eulogius, Bishop of Alexandria, and Anastasius, Bishop of Antioch.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: When the excellent preacher says, As long as I am the apostle of the Gentiles I will honour my ministry (Rom. xi.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 13); saying again in another place, We became as babes among you (1 Thess. ii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 7), he undoubtedly shews an example to us who come after him, that we should retain humility in our minds, and yet keep in honour the dignity of our order, so that neither should our humility be timid nor our elevation proud.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Now eight years ago, in the time of my predecessor of holy memory Pelagius, our brother and fellow-bishop John in the city of Constantinople, seeking occasion from another cause, held a synod in which he attempted to call himself Universal Bishop.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Which as soon as my said predecessor knew, he despatched letters annulling by the authority of the holy apostle Peter the acts of the said synod; of which letters I have taken care to send copies to your Holiness.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Moreover he forbade the deacon who attended us the most pious Lords for the business of the Church to celebrate the solemnities of mass with our aforesaid fellow-priest.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: I also, being of the same mind with him, have sent similar letters to our aforesaid fellow-priest, copies of which I have thought it right to send to your Blessedness, with this especial purpose, that we may first assail with moderate force the mind of our before-named brother concerning this matter, wherein by a new act of pride, all the bowels of the Universal Church are disturbed.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, if he should altogether refuse to be bent from the stiffness of his elation, then, with the succour of Almighty God, we may consider more particularly what ought to be done.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, as your venerable Holiness knows, this name of Universality was offered by the holy synod of Chalcedon to the pontiff of the Apostolic See which by the providence of God I serve.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But no one of my predecessors has ever consented to use this so profane a title; since, forsooth, if one Patriarch is called Universal, the name of Patriarch in the case of the rest is derogated.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But far be this, far be it from the mind of a Christian, that any one should wish to seize for himself that whereby he might seem in the least degree to lessen the honour of his brethren.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: While, then, we are unwilling to receive this honour when offered to us, think how disgraceful it is for any one to have wished to usurp it to himself perforce.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Wherefore let not your Holiness in your epistles ever call any one Universal, lest you detract from the honour due to yourself in offering to another what is not due.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Nor let any sinister suspicion make your mind uneasy with regard to our most serene lords, inasmuch as he fears Almighty God, and will in no way consent to do anything against the evangelical ordinances, against the most sacred canons.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: As for me, though separated from you by long spaces of land and sea, I am nevertheless entirely conjoined with you in heart.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And I trust that it is so in all respects with your Blessedness towards me; since, when you love me in return, you are not far from me.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Hence we give thanks the more to that grain of mustard seed (Matth. xiii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 31, 32), for that from what appeared a small and despicable seed it has been so spread abroad everywhere by branches rising and extending themselves from the same root that all the birds of heaven may make their nests in them.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And thanks be to that leaven which, in three measures of meal, has leavened in unity the mass of the whole human race (Matth. xiii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 33); and to the little stone, which, cut out of the mountain without hands, has occupied the whole face of the earth (Dan. ii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 35), and which to this end everywhere distends itself, that from the human race reduced to unity the body of the whole Church might be perfected, and so this distinction between the several members might serve for the benefit of the compacted whole.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Hence also we are not far from you, since in Him who is everywhere we are one.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Let us then give thanks to Him who, having abolished enmities, has caused that in His flesh there should be in the whole world one flock, and one sheepfold under Himself the one shepherd; and let us be ever mindful how the preacher of truth admonishes us, saying, Be careful to keep the unity of the spirit in the bond of peace (Ephes. iv.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 3), and, Follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see God (Hebr. xii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And he says also to other disciples, If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, having peace with all men (Rom. xii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For he sees that the good cannot have peace with the bad; and therefore, as ye know, he premised, If it be possible.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, because peace cannot be established except on two sides, when the bad fly from it, the good ought to keep it in their inmost hearts.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Whence also it is admirably said, As much as lieth in you; meaning that it should remain in us even when it is repelled from the hearts of evil men.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And such peace we truly keep, when we treat the faults of the proud at once with charity and with persistent justice, when we love them and hate their vices.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For man is the work of God; but vice is the work of man.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Let us then distinguish between what God and what man has made, and neither hate the man on account of his error nor love the error on account of the man.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Let us then with united mind attack the evil of pride in the man, that from his enemy, that is to say his error, the man himself may first be freed.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Our Almighty Redeemer will supply strength to charity and justice; He will supply to us, though placed far from each other, the unity of His Spirit; even He by whose workmanship the Church, having been constructed as it were after the manner of the ark with the four sides of the world, and bound together with the compacture of incorruptible planks and the pitch of charity, is disturbed by no opposing winds, by the swelling of no billow coming from without.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But inasmuch as, with His grace steering us, we ought to seek that no wave coming upon us from without may throw us into confusion, so ought we to pray with all our hearts, dearest brethren, that the right hand of His providence may draw out the accumulation of internal bilgewater within us.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For indeed our adversary the devil, who, in his rage against the humble, as a roaring lion walketh about seeking whom he may devour (1 Pet. v.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 8), no longer, as we perceive, walks about the folds but so resolutely fixes his teeth in certain necessary members of the Church that, unless with the favour of the Lord, the heedful crowd of shepherds unanimously run to the rescue, no one can doubt that he will soon tear all the sheepfold; which God forbid.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Consider, dearest brethren, who it is that follows close at hand, of whose approach such perverse beginnings are breaking out even in priests.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For it is because he is near of whom it is written, He is king over all the sons of pride (Job xli.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 25)—not without sore grief I am compelled to say it—that our brother and fellow-bishop John, despising the Lord’s commands, apostolical precepts, and rules of Fathers, attempts through elation to be his forerunner in name.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But may Almighty God make known to your Blessedness with what sore groaning I am tormented by this consideration; that he, the once to me most modest man, he who was beloved of all, he who seemed to be occupied in alms, deeds, prayers, and fastings, out of the ashes he sat in, out of the humility he preached, has grown so boastful as to attempt to claim all to himself, and through the elation of a pompous expression to aim at subjugating to himself all the members of Christ, which cohere to one Head only, that is to Christ.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Nor is it surprising that the same tempter who knows pride to be the beginning of all sin, who used it formerly before all else in the case of the first man, should now also put it before some men at the end of virtues, so as to lay it as a snare for those who to some extent seemed to be escaping his most cruel hands by the good aims of their life, at the very goal of good work, and as it were in the very conclusion of perfection.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Wherefore we ought to pray earnestly, and implore Almighty God with continual supplications, that He would avert this error from that man’s soul, and remove this mischief of pride and confusion from the unity and humility of the Church.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And with the favour of the Lord we ought to concur, and make provision with all our powers, lest in the poison of one expression the living members in the body of Christ should die.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, if this expression is suffered to be allowably used, the honour of all patriarchs is denied: and while he that is called Universal perishes per chance in his error, no bishop will be found to have remained in a state of truth.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: It is for you then, firmly and without prejudice, to keep the Churches as you have received them, and not to let this attempt at a diabolical usurpation have any countenance from you.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Stand firm; stand secure; presume not ever to issue or to receive writings with the falsity of the name Universal in them.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Bid all the bishops subject to your care abstain from the defilement of this elation, that the Universal Church may acknowledge you as Patriarchs not only in good works but also in the authority of truth.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, if perchance adversity is the consequence, we ought to persist unanimously, and show even by dying that in case of harm to the generality we do not love anything of our own especially.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Let us say with Paul, To me to live is Christ, and to die is gain (Philip. i.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Let us hear what the first of all pastors says; If ye suffer anything for righteousness’ sake, happy are ye (1 Pet. iii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For believe me that the dignity which we have received for the preaching of the truth we shall more safely relinquish than retain in behalf of the same truth, should case of necessity require it.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Finally, pray for me, as becomes your most dear Blessedness, that I may shew forth in works what I am thus bold to say to you.
